title: Which one does the compiler force you to handle?

mcq.question_md: A method opens a file with `new FileReader("marks.txt")`. The code refuses to compile until you add a `try`/`catch` or a `throws` clause. Which kind of exception causes this, and why?
options: Unchecked — the compiler flags all possible runtime failures
Checked — the compiler forces you to plan for outside-world failures like a missing file
An Error — file problems are JVM-level failures
Neither — this is only an IDE warning, and the code actually compiles
mcq.explanation_md: `FileNotFoundException` is a **checked** exception: a missing file is the outside world misbehaving, not a bug in your code, so the compiler makes you decide upfront — catch it or declare `throws`. The tempting wrong answer is the first one: unchecked exceptions (`NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`) are usually bugs in your own logic, and the compiler stays completely silent about them — if it flagged every possible runtime failure, every line would need a catch block.
